title: Biohacking and Energy Management
subtitle: Body, Sleep, Nutrition, and Mental Stamina
description: A foundational checklist on how the human body works and how to manage its resources. Without physical health and stable energy, any ambition in other areas loses meaning. Here are scientifically grounded principles for optimizing the body and brain.

items.title: Circadian Rhythms and Sleep
items.text: Sleep is not passive rest, but an active process of brain cleansing from toxins and memory consolidation. Chronic sleep deprivation (less than 7 hours) increases risk of dementia, reduces testosterone, and disrupts glucose metabolism. One hour of sleep before midnight equals two hours after in restorative value.
items.tip: Wake up and go to bed at the same time every day, including weekends. This sets your internal clock.

items.title: Morning Light
items.text: The first thing the brain should see after waking is bright natural light (even on overcast days). This triggers timely cortisol production and sets the timer for evening melatonin production. Without this signal, the circadian rhythm is disrupted.
items.tip: Within 30 minutes of waking, go out on the balcony or outside for 5-10 minutes without sunglasses.

items.title: Delayed Caffeine
items.text: Adenosine — the fatigue molecule — accumulates overnight. Caffeine doesn't give energy; it simply blocks adenosine receptors. If you drink coffee immediately after waking, an acute energy crash occurs by midday. The body needs 90-120 minutes to naturally 'wake up.'
items.tip: Drink your first coffee or tea 1.5-2 hours after waking.

items.title: Zone 2 Cardio
items.text: For longevity and metabolic health, long-duration, low-intensity 'Zone 2' training is more important than high-intensity training to exhaustion. In this heart rate range (120-140 bpm), mitochondria learn to efficiently use fat as fuel.
items.tip: Train at a heart rate where you can maintain a conversation but don't want to sing.

items.title: Strength Training and Aging
items.text: After age 30, a person loses 3-8% of muscle mass per decade (sarcopenia). Muscles are not only for strength but also a metabolic organ regulating blood sugar. Strength training is the only proven way to slow this process.
items.tip: Incorporate basic multi-joint exercises: squats, pulls, presses.

items.title: Parasympathetic Activation
items.text: Chronic stress keeps the nervous system in 'fight or flight' mode. Without conscious activation of the 'rest' mode (parasympathetic), the body cannot recover. Breathing is the only autonomic process we can consciously control.
items.tip: Practice the physiological sigh: double inhale through the nose and long exhale through the mouth. This lowers heart rate in 30 seconds.

items.title: Protein Timing and MPS
items.text: Muscle Protein Synthesis (MPS) — the tissue renewal process — is most active in the first hours after exercise, but it needs building materials. Distributing protein evenly throughout the day (30-40g per meal) is more effective than one large dinner.
items.tip: Aim for 1.6g of protein per kg of body weight daily.

items.title: Dopamine Fasting Before Sleep
items.text: Blue light from screens suppresses melatonin, but the main problem is dopamine stimulation from content. Scrolling feeds before sleep keeps the brain in an excited novelty-seeking state, preventing transition into deep sleep.
items.tip: Put your phone in another room an hour before bed, or activate black-and-white screen mode.

items.title: Thermoregulation for Sleep
items.text: For sleep onset, core body temperature must drop by 1-2 degrees. A bedroom that's too warm is a common cause of insomnia. A warm shower before bed paradoxically helps, as afterward blood vessels dilate and heat is released to the environment.

items.title: Neuroplasticity and Focus
items.text: The brain learns only in a state of heightened concentration followed by deep rest. 90-minute sessions of maximum focus work followed by a period of 'mind-wandering' is the optimal cycle for memory and creativity.
items.tip: Work in cycles: 90 minutes focus / 20 minutes rest without phone.

quiz.q: How long after waking is it recommended to drink your first coffee?

a: Working at a heart rate where you can talk
